title: Due Date Calculator — Calculate Your Baby's Due Date by LMP or Conception
description: Calculate your pregnancy due date using last menstrual period, conception date, or IVF transfer date. Get trimester dates and key milestones. Free & accurate.
ymyl_disclaimer: Due date calculations are estimates based on Naegele's rule. Actual birth may occur 2–3 weeks before or after the estimated date. Ultrasound measurements are more accurate for pregnancy dating. Always follow your healthcare provider's guidance.

faq.title: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.q1: How is the due date calculated from the last menstrual period?
faq.a1: The standard due date calculation uses Naegele's rule: add 280 days (40 weeks) to the first day of your last menstrual period (LMP). The formula was developed by German obstetrician Franz Karl Naegele in the early 1800s and remains the standard in obstetric practice today. It assumes a regular 28-day menstrual cycle with ovulation on day 14. For example, an LMP of January 1, 2025 gives an estimated due date of October 8, 2025. The 40-week count is somewhat counterintuitive — it includes the 2 weeks before conception, which is why gestational age is always approximately 2 weeks more than fetal age.
faq.q2: How accurate is the estimated due date?
faq.a2: Only approximately 4% of babies are born on their exact estimated due date. A normal full-term delivery falls anywhere from 37 to 42 weeks gestation — a 5-week window around the due date. Early ultrasound (before 13 weeks) is more accurate than LMP dating, with a margin of error of ±5–7 days for first-trimester scans versus ±2 weeks for LMP-based calculation. This is because early fetal growth is highly consistent regardless of maternal factors. After the first trimester, normal variation in fetal size makes ultrasound dating progressively less accurate. For women with irregular cycles, longer cycles, or uncertain LMP dates, early ultrasound dating is strongly recommended.
faq.q3: How do IVF due dates differ from natural conception?
faq.a3: IVF (in vitro fertilization) pregnancies allow precise dating because the exact age of the embryo is known at transfer. For a day-3 embryo transfer, the embryo is already 3 days old, so the due date = transfer date + 263 days (rather than 266 days from conception). For a day-5 blastocyst transfer, due date = transfer date + 261 days. The LMP equivalent date (used to align with obstetric dating conventions) is calculated differently: for day-3 transfer, LMP equivalent = transfer date − 17 days; for day-5 transfer, LMP equivalent = transfer date − 19 days. These adjusted calculations are more accurate than LMP dating for IVF pregnancies and are used by reproductive endocrinologists and OB/GYNs.
faq.q4: What is the difference between gestational age and fetal age?
faq.a4: Gestational age is the standard clinical measurement, counted from the first day of the last menstrual period — not from conception. Fetal age (embryonic age) counts from conception, which typically occurs around day 14 of the cycle. This means gestational age is approximately 2 weeks more than fetal age throughout pregnancy. At a gestational age of 12 weeks, the fetus is actually about 10 weeks old. All obstetric milestones, trimester boundaries, and fetal development charts use gestational age — not fetal age — because LMP is always known while the exact conception date often is not.
faq.q5: When does each trimester begin and end?
faq.a5: The first trimester spans weeks 1 through 13 of gestational age — from LMP through the end of the 13th week. This phase includes embryonic development and organogenesis; the risk of miscarriage is highest in this period (approximately 10–15% of recognized pregnancies). The second trimester covers weeks 14 through 27 — typically the most physically comfortable period. The anatomy ultrasound is usually performed around week 20. The third trimester begins at week 28 and continues through birth, normally at week 40. Babies born between 37 and 42 weeks are considered full-term; those born before 37 weeks are preterm.
faq.q6: What are the key pregnancy milestones I should know about?
faq.a6: Major obstetric milestones by gestational week: Week 6–8: First prenatal visit, heartbeat detectable by transvaginal ultrasound. Week 10–13: First trimester screening (nuchal translucency ultrasound + blood tests for chromosomal conditions). Week 12: End of highest miscarriage risk period. Week 18–22: Anatomy scan (detailed ultrasound checking fetal development). Week 24: Fetal viability threshold — survival outside the womb becomes possible with intensive neonatal care. Week 28: Third trimester begins; RhoGAM injection for Rh-negative mothers. Week 36: Weekly prenatal visits typically begin. Week 37: Full-term threshold. Week 40: Estimated due date. Week 41–42: Post-term; monitoring and potential induction discussions.
faq.q7: What happens if my cycle is not 28 days?
faq.a7: Naegele's rule assumes a 28-day cycle with ovulation on day 14. For cycles longer than 28 days, ovulation occurs later, and the due date should be adjusted forward. For cycles shorter than 28 days, the due date should be adjusted backward. The adjustment is approximately: Due Date = Standard Naegele Due Date + (Actual Cycle Length − 28 days). For a 32-day cycle: add 4 days to the standard due date. For a 25-day cycle: subtract 3 days. This adjustment is one reason why early ultrasound dating often differs from LMP-based calculation — ultrasound accounts for actual fetal size rather than assumed cycle length.
faq.q8: What is the difference between preterm, full-term, and post-term birth?
faq.a8: Preterm birth is delivery before 37 completed weeks of gestation. Subtypes: extremely preterm (<28 weeks), very preterm (28–31 weeks), moderate to late preterm (32–36 weeks). Preterm birth affects approximately 10% of pregnancies worldwide and is the leading cause of newborn morbidity and mortality. Full-term birth is 37 to 42 weeks. The American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists further defines: early term (37–38 weeks), full term (39–40 weeks), and late term (41 weeks) — recognizing that 39–40 weeks provides the best outcomes for most babies. Post-term birth is beyond 42 weeks. Most providers initiate fetal monitoring and consider induction at 41 weeks due to increasing placental insufficiency risk beyond this point.

content.whatIs:
A due date calculator estimates your baby's expected birth date using one of four methods: last menstrual period (LMP), known conception date, IVF day-3 embryo transfer, or IVF day-5 blastocyst transfer. It applies the standard obstetric formula — Naegele's rule for natural conception, or embryo-age-adjusted formulas for IVF — to produce the estimated due date, current gestational age, trimester, days remaining, and a timeline of key pregnancy milestones.

The estimated due date (EDD) is one of the first and most important pieces of information established in prenatal care. It defines the timeline for all subsequent care: when screenings should be performed, when the fetus reaches viability, when to expect delivery, and when interventions may be appropriate. While only about 4% of births occur on the exact EDD, the 40-week framework around which the EDD is centered guides the structure of the entire pregnancy.

Understanding gestational age — the standard clinical measure counted from LMP — is essential for interpreting all pregnancy information, from apps and books to medical reports. 'You are 8 weeks pregnant' means 8 weeks from LMP, which is only about 6 weeks of actual fetal development. This consistent offset between gestational and fetal age affects how parents interpret size charts, developmental milestones, and the meaning of prenatal screening results.
content.formula:
From Last Menstrual Period (LMP):
  Due Date = LMP + 280 days (40 weeks)
  Estimated Conception = LMP + 14 days

From Known Conception Date:
  Due Date = Conception + 266 days
  LMP equivalent = Conception − 14 days

From IVF Day-3 Embryo Transfer:
  Due Date = Transfer Date + 263 days
  LMP equivalent = Transfer Date − 17 days

From IVF Day-5 Blastocyst Transfer:
  Due Date = Transfer Date + 261 days
  LMP equivalent = Transfer Date − 19 days

Gestational Age = (Today − LMP equivalent) ÷ 7 weeks + remainder days

Trimester Boundaries:
  First:  Weeks 1–13 (LMP to LMP+91 days)
  Second: Weeks 14–27 (LMP+92 to LMP+189 days)
  Third:  Weeks 28+ (LMP+190 days to birth)
content.example:
Example 1 — LMP method:
LMP: January 10, 2025
Due date: October 17, 2025 (280 days later)
Estimated conception: January 24, 2025
At week 20 (May 23): Anatomy scan due. Second trimester.
At week 37 (September 19): Full-term threshold reached.

Example 2 — IVF day-5 blastocyst transfer:
Transfer date: February 3, 2025
Due date: February 3 + 261 days = October 22, 2025
LMP equivalent: February 3 − 19 days = January 15, 2025
Gestational age at transfer: 5 weeks exactly (5+0).

Example 3 — Cycle length adjustment:
LMP: January 1, 2025. Cycle length: 35 days (not 28).
Standard Naegele due date: October 8, 2025
Adjusted due date: October 8 + 7 days = October 15, 2025
Ultrasound at 12 weeks will confirm actual dating.
content.howItWorks_1: Select your calculation method from the dropdown. Last Menstrual Period (LMP) is the most common for natural conception — you need the exact date of the first day of your most recent period. Conception Date is used if you know precisely when conception occurred. IVF Day-3 and Day-5 options are for embryo transfer pregnancies where the embryo age is precisely known.
content.howItWorks_2: Enter the relevant date using the date picker. For LMP method, enter the first day of your last period — not the last day or a middle day. Click Calculate to run the obstetric formula appropriate for your selected method.
content.howItWorks_3: Review your complete pregnancy summary: Due Date, current Gestational Age in weeks and days, which Trimester you are in, Days Remaining until the due date (or days overdue if past it), estimated Conception Date, LMP equivalent date, and the full milestone timeline. Milestones marked as 'Past' have already occurred; 'Upcoming' shows what to expect next in your pregnancy journey.
content.whyItMatters:
The estimated due date is more than a calendar target — it is the reference point for the entire structure of prenatal care. Every screening, test, and developmental milestone in pregnancy is scheduled relative to gestational age, which is anchored to the EDD. The first-trimester nuchal translucency screening must be performed between weeks 11 and 14 — outside this window, the measurement is not accurate. The anatomy scan at week 20 is timed when fetal anatomy is large enough to assess in detail. Group B Streptococcus (GBS) testing occurs at weeks 36–37. Understanding your EDD precisely ensures you receive these screenings within their optimal windows.

For many families, the EDD is also the first organizing date of early parenthood — maternity and paternity leave requests, childcare waitlists (which often have 6–12 month waiting periods), hospital pre-registration, and childbirth education classes are all coordinated around it. Planning for a birth 3 weeks before or after the EDD is reasonable, given that only 4% of babies arrive on the exact date. Many experienced parents and midwives describe the due date as the center of a 'due month' rather than a precise target.

In the context of IVF and assisted reproduction, precise dating is particularly important because IVF pregnancies involve exact knowledge of embryo age that natural conception cannot provide. IVF-specific calculations have been validated in large clinical studies and are consistently more accurate than LMP-based dating for IVF pregnancies. Combining IVF transfer date with the appropriate embryo age adjustment (day-3 or day-5) gives the most precise gestational age calculation available — often more accurate than even first-trimester ultrasound.
content.limitations:
Naegele's rule assumes a regular 28-day menstrual cycle with ovulation on day 14 — conditions that apply to only about 30% of women. Women with irregular cycles, PCOS (which can cause irregular ovulation), recent hormonal contraception use, or recent pregnancy or breastfeeding may have ovulation at significantly different times. For these individuals, LMP-based dating is less reliable, and first-trimester ultrasound dating is recommended to establish the EDD accurately.

This calculator cannot detect or account for variations in fetal growth rate, placental function, or medical conditions affecting pregnancy. The EDD produced here is a statistical estimate — normal birth occurs anywhere between 37 and 42 weeks. Some complications (intrauterine growth restriction, pre-eclampsia, placenta previa) may result in medically indicated preterm delivery before the EDD. Conversely, post-dates pregnancies (beyond 42 weeks) carry increasing risks and typically prompt clinical intervention. The calculator provides information for planning purposes only — all clinical decisions should be made with your OB/GYN or midwife.

This calculator is not a medical device and does not replace prenatal ultrasound for dating accuracy. If your care provider's ultrasound-derived EDD differs from the calculator's LMP-based EDD by more than 7 days in the first trimester, clinical practice generally adjusts the EDD to match the ultrasound measurement — the more accurate method. Follow your healthcare provider's established EDD for all clinical care decisions.
content.tips_1: Record the first day of your last menstrual period as soon as possible — this is the single most important date in establishing your EDD and gestational age for all subsequent prenatal care. If you are trying to conceive, begin tracking your cycle with a period-tracking app or calendar. An uncertain LMP is one of the most common reasons for discrepancy between calculator-based and ultrasound-based dating.
content.tips_2: Book your first prenatal appointment as early as possible — ideally at 6–8 weeks gestation (4–6 weeks after your missed period). Early prenatal care establishes accurate dating via ultrasound, screens for risk factors, begins important supplementation (folic acid, prenatal vitamins), and sets the schedule for all subsequent screenings. Delaying the first appointment until 12 weeks risks missing the optimal window for first-trimester genetic screening.
content.tips_3: For IVF pregnancies, use the specific IVF transfer date and embryo type (day-3 or day-5) for the most accurate due date calculation. The IVF-specific formulas in this calculator are more accurate than LMP-based calculation because embryo age is precisely known. Confirm the calculated EDD with your reproductive endocrinologist — they will have this documented from your treatment cycle.
content.tips_4: Plan for a 'due window' of ±2 weeks around your EDD rather than a fixed date. This means having hospital bag ready by week 37, childcare and parental leave arrangements confirmed by week 36, and post-dates monitoring discussions with your provider beginning around week 40–41. Babies who arrive at 38 or 42 weeks are both entirely normal — a flexible mindset around the EDD reduces stress and improves preparedness.
